name: Basque (Euskara)

sandhi_rules.name: ez+d→ezt (regressive devoicing)
sandhi_rules.notes: ez + d- → ezt-: the voiced /d/ of the following word devoices to [t] after the laminal sibilant of ez while the sibilant is retained — ez dut → eztut [es̻ tut], ez da → ezta [es̻ ta], ez dakit → eztakit [es̻ takit]. Categorical connected-speech sandhi of the negative particle ez /es̻/ before a following auxiliary/verb-initial consonant, obligatory for all speakers of Standard Basque and pan-Basque across the dialects (the DEGREE/spelling of the assimilation varies dialectally, but the contractions themselves are general): Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, Hualde 'Segmental phonology' §2 (consonant assimilation / sandhi of the negative particle), pp.15-65; Hualde 1991, Basque Phonology, §on the negative particle ez. Modelled on eu (Batua) and inherited by the historical-dialect specs via the id-keyed sandhi overlay; a dialect that does not realise ez as /es̻/ simply does not fire the rule (null beats a wrong contraction).

sandhi_rules.name: ez+z→etz (sibilant→affricate coalescence)
sandhi_rules.notes: ez + z- → etz-: the sibilant of ez coalesces with the following laminal sibilant into the laminal affricate [ts̻], leaving the vowel of ez bare — ez zen → etzen [e ts̻en], ez zara → etzara [e ts̻aɾa]. Categorical connected-speech sandhi of the negative particle ez /es̻/ before a following auxiliary/verb-initial consonant, obligatory for all speakers of Standard Basque and pan-Basque across the dialects (the DEGREE/spelling of the assimilation varies dialectally, but the contractions themselves are general): Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, Hualde 'Segmental phonology' §2 (consonant assimilation / sandhi of the negative particle), pp.15-65; Hualde 1991, Basque Phonology, §on the negative particle ez. Modelled on eu (Batua) and inherited by the historical-dialect specs via the id-keyed sandhi overlay; a dialect that does not realise ez as /es̻/ simply does not fire the rule (null beats a wrong contraction).

sandhi_rules.name: ez+n→en (sibilant deletion before nasal)
sandhi_rules.notes: ez + n- → en-: the sibilant of ez is deleted before a following nasal — ez naiz → enaiz [e nais̻], ez nator → enator [e natoɾ]. Categorical connected-speech sandhi of the negative particle ez /es̻/ before a following auxiliary/verb-initial consonant, obligatory for all speakers of Standard Basque and pan-Basque across the dialects (the DEGREE/spelling of the assimilation varies dialectally, but the contractions themselves are general): Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, Hualde 'Segmental phonology' §2 (consonant assimilation / sandhi of the negative particle), pp.15-65; Hualde 1991, Basque Phonology, §on the negative particle ez. Modelled on eu (Batua) and inherited by the historical-dialect specs via the id-keyed sandhi overlay; a dialect that does not realise ez as /es̻/ simply does not fire the rule (null beats a wrong contraction).

sandhi_rules.name: ez+l→el (sibilant deletion before lateral)
sandhi_rules.notes: ez + l- → el-: the sibilant of ez is deleted before a following lateral — ez luke → eluke [e luke], ez litzateke → elitzateke [e lits̻ateke]. Categorical connected-speech sandhi of the negative particle ez /es̻/ before a following auxiliary/verb-initial consonant, obligatory for all speakers of Standard Basque and pan-Basque across the dialects (the DEGREE/spelling of the assimilation varies dialectally, but the contractions themselves are general): Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, Hualde 'Segmental phonology' §2 (consonant assimilation / sandhi of the negative particle), pp.15-65; Hualde 1991, Basque Phonology, §on the negative particle ez. Modelled on eu (Batua) and inherited by the historical-dialect specs via the id-keyed sandhi overlay; a dialect that does not realise ez as /es̻/ simply does not fire the rule (null beats a wrong contraction).

sandhi_rules.name: ez+b→ezp (regressive devoicing)
sandhi_rules.notes: ez + b- → ezp-: the voiced /b/ of the following word devoices to [p] after the sibilant of ez, which is retained — ez balitz → ezpalitz [es̻ palits̻], ez bada → ezpada [es̻ pada]. Categorical connected-speech sandhi of the negative particle ez /es̻/ before a following auxiliary/verb-initial consonant, obligatory for all speakers of Standard Basque and pan-Basque across the dialects (the DEGREE/spelling of the assimilation varies dialectally, but the contractions themselves are general): Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, Hualde 'Segmental phonology' §2 (consonant assimilation / sandhi of the negative particle), pp.15-65; Hualde 1991, Basque Phonology, §on the negative particle ez. Modelled on eu (Batua) and inherited by the historical-dialect specs via the id-keyed sandhi overlay; a dialect that does not realise ez as /es̻/ simply does not fire the rule (null beats a wrong contraction).

sandhi_rules.name: ez+g→ezk (regressive devoicing)
sandhi_rules.notes: ez + g- → ezk-: the voiced /ɡ/ of the following word devoices to [k] after the sibilant of ez, which is retained — ez gara → ezkara [es̻ kaɾa], ez gaitu → ezkaitu [es̻ kaitu]. Categorical connected-speech sandhi of the negative particle ez /es̻/ before a following auxiliary/verb-initial consonant, obligatory for all speakers of Standard Basque and pan-Basque across the dialects (the DEGREE/spelling of the assimilation varies dialectally, but the contractions themselves are general): Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, Hualde 'Segmental phonology' §2 (consonant assimilation / sandhi of the negative particle), pp.15-65; Hualde 1991, Basque Phonology, §on the negative particle ez. Modelled on eu (Batua) and inherited by the historical-dialect specs via the id-keyed sandhi overlay; a dialect that does not realise ez as /es̻/ simply does not fire the rule (null beats a wrong contraction).

notes: Standard Basque (Euskara Batua), codified by the Royal Academy of the Basque Language (Euskaltzaindia) from 1968. Conservative Basque varieties have a three-way sibilant place contrast: lamino-alveolar /s̻/ (spelled z), apico-alveolar /s̺/ (spelled s) and postalveolar /ʃ/ (spelled x), with the matching affricates /ts̻/ (tz), /ts̺/ (ts) and /tʃ/ (tx); this three-way contrast is fully stable in eastern and Navarrese varieties such as Goizueta but is neutralised further west (Hualde, Lujanbio & Zubiri 2010: 119). Palatal stops /c/ (tt) and /ɟ/ (dd), palatal nasal /ɲ/ (ñ) and palatal lateral /ʎ/ (ll) arise historically by palatalisation of /t d n l/ after /i/. Voiced stops /b d ɡ/ are realised as approximants [β ð ɣ] intervocalically (Hualde et al. 2010: 116), and nasals assimilate in place to a following consonant (2010: 120). Five vowels /i e a o u/. Aspiration (/h/, aspirated stops) is preserved only in the eastern (continental) dialects and was lost in the west (Hualde 2018: 2). Dialectal variation across the historical dialects is extensive; Batua draws primarily from Gipuzkoan and Lapurdian sources. Basque is a language isolate with no demonstrated genetic relationship to any other language family. Single ⟨r⟩ is a tap [ɾ] in the onset — intervocalically and in obstruent+r clusters (⟨tr pr kr gr br dr fr⟩) — but the tap/trill contrast is neutralised in the syllable coda, where it surfaces as the trill [r]: word-finally (⟨eder⟩ [eder], ⟨gaur⟩ [ɡau̯r]) and before a consonant (⟨bernart⟩ [bernart], ⟨korpus⟩ [korpus̺]); ⟨rr⟩ is always the trill (Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003). The falling diphthongs ⟨ai ei oi au eu⟩ are tautosyllabic with a non-syllabic offglide [i̯ u̯]. Coda /n/ before a labial /b p m/ assimilates in place to [m] (⟨anbulantzia⟩ [ambulants̻ia]; Hualde, Lujanbio & Zubiri 2010: 120). STRESS EXEMPTION (research/production tier rule): Basque accent is dialect-variable (pitch-accent in some western varieties, weak demarcative stress elsewhere; Hualde 1999) and never marked in Batua orthography, so the spec carries no stress block per docs/quality_tiers.md.

sources.author: Hualde, J.I. & Ortiz de Urbina, J. (eds.)
sources.title: A Grammar of Basque

sources.author: Hualde, J.I., Lujanbio, O. & Zubiri, J.J.
sources.title: Goizueta Basque (Illustrations of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association 40(1)
sources.notes: Read for the base sibilant/affricate system (p.119), voiced-stop spirantization (p.116), nasal assimilation and lateral delateralization (p.120), and the five-vowel system (p.122).

sources.author: Bedialauneta Txurruka, I. & Hualde, J.I.
sources.title: Markina Basque (Illustration of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association 53(3)
sources.notes: Read for the Western/Biscayan sibilant and palatal-stop mergers (p.1098) and for the note that Zuberoan has a three-way plosive contrast (p.1099).

sources.author: Hualde, J.I.
sources.title: Aspiration in Basque
sources.notes: Read for the east/west distribution of /h/ and aspirated stops (pp.1-3, 84 fn.) and the Zuberoan three-way plosive contrast (p.2).

sources.author: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I.
sources.title: The Sounds of the World's Languages

sources.author: Hualde, J.I.
sources.title: Basque Phonology
sources.pages: ch. 'The negative particle ez' / consonant assimilation sandhi
sources.notes: Read for the categorical connected-speech contractions of the negative particle ez (ez+d→ezt, ez+z→etz, ez+n→en, ez+l→el, ez+b→ezp, ez+g→ezk), the reference statement of pan-Basque ez-sandhi with dialectal variation in degree.

ancestors.notes: Latin/Romance adstrate: two millennia of contact left a very large lexical layer.
Spanish adstrate: modern loanwords and phonological pressure.
French adstrate: on the northern (Iparralde) dialects.

allophone_rules.notes: Nasal place assimilation (Hualde, Lujanbio & Zubiri 2010:120): coda /n/ before a labial /b p m/ surfaces as [m], e.g. anbulantzia [ambulants̩ia], denbora [dembora].
